sentences.text: Well, this particular-- I hadn't gone down to the police headquarters to-- to report other murders that had occurred in Detroit.
I don't remember particularly what this particular murder was, but it's the-- getting to police headquarters that night.
I-- it was Saturday night.
I knew they needed me down there.
It was 2:00 in the morning and I-- I walked out onto-- I could not get a cab.
I phoned for a cab.
And mind you, up to this time, because of my age, 19, the police wanted to come out and pick me up and take me to police headquarters.
But I-- I did not.
I said I'll get there in cabs.
And I stepped right out into Woodward Avenue, which was a wide-laned major thoroughfare.
And I literally stopped a cab in the street at 2:00 in the morning with a passenger.
And I told him that I absolutely had to get to police headquarters as fast as possible.
I was a court reporter.
There was a murder, and could-- would he be willing to take me with his passenger?
So he did, indeed.
But he was going down to the Statler Hotel with this passenger in downtown Detroit.
And I told him that would be fine.
I would call police headquarters from there, and I would tell them I'm at the Statler.
You'll have to send a car, a detective, to get me, which they always wanted to do anyway.
So I went into the hotel, made the phone call.
And by that time it was about 2:45 AM.
And they said they were sending over a detective right away to pick me up, which was not very far from police headquarters.
So I stepped out of the Statler Hotel and walked down the big steps.
And I was watching-- I knew it wouldn't take them five minutes to get there.
And I was watching for the dark green detective's-- unmarked detective's car to come around the corner from the left on a one-way street.
And here came around the corner this dark green car-- I think they were Chevrolets-- the same car that the police department used.
So I bounded right down, opened that door, and got in.
And after I got in, I smelled a-- an-- an odor of alcohol.
I looked at the dashboard of the car, and there was no-- there were no large gold letters on the dashboard indicating a police car.
In the unmarked police cars, they were on the dashboard.
I looked at this man, who was an enormous man with a huge scar across his cheek, and I knew I was in the wrong car.
And he started right up.
And I thought-- and I said to him-- I knew I was in the wrong car, and I said, well you've got to get me to police headquarters right away, because they're expecting me.
I wanted him to know that.
And he said, why?
In this very guttural voice.
Was there a murder in the hotel?
And I said, no, but there was a murder somewhere.
And I didn't know where I was at that moment, but it-- there was a circle around the hotel area there.
And I could see the stop light coming up at the next quarter of this circle.
And he had to stop at that stop light.
And I was down on the handle of that door.
And he grabbed my-- my coat as I was pulling out of it and just yelled at me, where do you think you're going, babe?
And he literally pulled the sleeve loose on my coat as I was getting out.
But I had the coat.
And I ran all the way back to that hotel.
And I was standing out-- I-- I waited at the doors inside.
And around the corner, same corner, came the dark green police car-- detective's car.
And I went down and got in it.
And-- I mean, I recognized the detective and I told him what had happened.
So after that they insisted on coming to get me.
However, you know, this was all part of that job of being a court reporter.
I have no idea.
But you know, there-- there-- I was reporting in federal court in Detroit.
The big mafia trials too-- in federal court during that time.
Oh, yeah, I-- I wouldn't have been surprised.
I think the man looked very much to be a mafia type.
The race riots occurred before I got there.
I was just aware of this.

And I-- I had been told that the riots were-- were initiated through a lie by whites involved, implicating-- claiming that a black had done something, that he had not done, which was the instigation of this terrible race riot in Detroit.
I do not-- I do not remember.
It seems as though in Detroit, we-- I reported an awful lot of cases at the prosecutor's office during the night, involving blacks and whites.
And it seemed that the-- the blacks always-- seemed-- not always but for the most part, had to be provoked.
Had to be provoked.
And that's about all I remember.
I don't remember much of anything else.
Yes.
After I became a court reporter in Detroit, then we had the big Detroit-- the I think was the paper.
And of course, we were getting national, international news.
And I remember a great deal of publicity about the-- what was going on in Germany.
Not about-- not the extent to which we know now, but at least the war.
I mean, it seemed as though there were a great-- great-- an unusually high number of-- of young men from my high school class in Woodstock, Illinois, who were in that war and who were killed.
By the time, I was well into my reporting work in Detroit.
So I knew, you know, that-- about the wars.
And it-- it hit a personal-- it hit me personally because of the people I knew who were killed at Pearl Harbor and were on the So--
I don't have a recollection, specifically, of atrocities.
I knew that there-- that-- I knew about the war.
And I was following-- following this.
I want to go back to [?
Graig ?] College.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also I want to say that I went to movies.
And on the Movietone newsreels-- no one, of course, had any television at that time until about 1948.
But in the Movietone newsreels, we were getting a lot of information about the wars.
But I don't remember anything specifically about atrocities.
This was 1944 and '45-- no, 1945.
But going back to [?
Graig ?] College, I developed a friendship in [?
Graig ?] College with a German girl named Huber and-- in the court reporting class.
She was born and raised in Germany up to age 16.
And she and her parents were living in Chicago.
But we became very good friends.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She invited me to spend one weekend at her parents' apartment.
I'm relating this story to give you an idea of awareness of what was going on-- my awareness.
And I spent that weekend in her parents' apartment in Chicago.
She told me-- she spoke very beautiful English.
She told me that her parents did not speak English, and that she would interpret any conversation that we had between us, which would be, obviously, very minimal.
She showed me pictures of her growing up in Germany with all of her friends, who were in Nazi uniforms.
And some pictures, she had cut off the bottom part from the shoulder down, because she said that she did not want to have these pictures of her friends with Nazi uniforms on.
The youth-- the-- the young group in Germany--
The Nazi Youth, yes.
And there was another name for them.
But I thought that was kind of interesting.
I knew she-- We didn't talk much about her life in Germany.
But while I was in her-- their apartment that weekend, the radio was on, and Hitler had some great something.
I cannot attribute it to any of his marches into Poland, which was 1939.
This was 1943-- '43 when I was in [?
Graig ?] College before I went to Detroit.
But whatever it was, it was a huge quote, 'victory,' unquote, for Hitler.
Those two parents were so excited and happy for people who did not understand English.
And I thought, there is something strange here going on.
And it wasn't until I got to Nuremberg-- she was the first person that I saw in the Grand Hotel, or in the courthouse-- courthouse when I arrived.
She had gotten back to Germany.
She was a US citizen then, as a court reporter.
And it was only through a British-- a BBC correspondent, an American, that I learned that her father was the head of the Chicago Bund in Chicago.
His name was Huber.
In our freelance reporting office, into our office came a flyer-- a printed flyer from the United States War Department, recruiting court reporters.
And this must have been in 19-- the early part of 1945.
The war ended on May 8th, '45, and they were going to go to trial in Germany on the major Nazi leaders in November.
But that date had not been set.
They knew they needed a lot of court reporters.

So these flyers went out, I understood, into every major court reporting office in major cities of the United States, the large cities, recruiting approximately 26 court reporters to go to Germany to report Goering's trial, get started.
I understood later that other reporters in other cities did not know about it, because the principals in their offices did not want to lose them to Germany or Japan and for the Japanese war trials.
So they did not let them have the knowledge of this flyer which was sent.
But my boss did.
He let us know about it.
And I applied immediately.
Well, by that time, I had become intrigued with what was going on, maybe stemming from my friendship with that German in [?
Graig ?] College.
My mother was German, and all my family.
And somehow I just had a feeling that I needed to go to Germany and work on these trials.
Because, by that time, 1945, knowledge about these massacres was coming out in the press.
So I applied.
And I knew I had the speed.
I had the skill.
I especially had this-- this specialty in medical terminology that I had in court reporting.
And I-- I got a response from the War Department that I was well qualified in all areas except I was too young.
I was only 20, and I had to be 21.
So I knew I just had to wait until I was 21.
In the meantime, they had gotten-- they had recruited their reporters to get over there and start Goering's trial.
Some of them were from the military, court reporters who were already in Europe.
And-- but they recruited most of them as civilians who were old enough.
So--
Soon as I was 21, they told me I could go.
And when I was 21, of course, by that time the reporters were already there for Goering's trial.

And at the processing and my getting cholera and tetanus shots and passing the US Civil Service Examination at 200 words per minute, that was the high, you know, the required speed, I-- I was accepted.
Signed a contract for one year-- until my memory came to recently, I felt that it was a two-year contract, but it was a one year.
I remember saying-- thinking if I signed up for a one year, I could always extend it.
And did.
Not at all, other then we had begun-- begun to see on Movietone newsreels the pictures of devastated German cities, Nuremberg included.
So I knew I was going to be going to a bombed out city.
That's all.

But in my office in Detroit, there were two other court reporters who, and when I was 20 years old, they were-- one was 30 and one was 40, and who were accepted-- applied and were accepted, too, who actually preceded me.
Then one of my favorite judges on the Detroit bench, Robert Toms, applied as a judge and was accepted.
And an attorney I knew, Murphy, George Murphy, who was the retainer for the University of Michigan attorney applied and was accepted.
So we had our own little-- I could see a-- a Michigan contingent going, and I was going to be the last one because I wasn't old enough.
No, not at all, because we had no idea.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
None at all.
And I-- I-- Michigan at that time was a non-capital punishment state.

So here was this great judge, Robert Toms, going from that state to a situation in Germany-- which was interesting, talking to him about it afterwards-- where he had absolutely no problem with verdicts-- sentences of death.
Pardon me.
Orientation?
No.
Not really, no.
Orders were cut, and I was assigned to the Office of Chief of Council for War Crimes, Nuremberg, Germany.
I-- and I received those orders at the end of October in 1946, which was over a year after I had originally-- even after I became 21, it took that long, you know.
They had the next group of reporters to get, because many of those-- most of them who reported the major Nazi leaders trial, Goering's trial, did not stay on.
Some did, to report the 12 subsequent proceedings.
Civilian employee of the War Department, subject to the military and all that goes with it, including court's martial-- or court martial.
Right.
I received my orders.
And I packed my mother's trunk with clothes that I would-- I mean, as many clothes as I could get, and other-- anything that you would pack to go on a trip for a year.
And I-- I don't remember-- that was shipped by surface, of course, not flown.
And I-- I flew from-- on a military plane out of Detroit to Westover Field, Massachusetts, which was what my orders said I was to do.

And at Westover Field Military Base, from where all these planes were flying to Paris and Germany, at-- at Westover, I was billeted and told it would probably be about six days before I would be flying out.
And--
So it was a military base.
It was the end of October.
And I became acquainted with some GIs there who also were going to be going to Germany on their tours of duty.
And they invited me to their base Halloween party the next evening.
And I remember driving with them down to Holyoke, Massachusetts, for-- to pick up some of the girls there to bring back to go to this Halloween party.
And when the party was over-- and it was a lot of fun-- we-- I went-- got back to my billet at 1:00 AM.
And at 2:00 AM, I was awakened and told to be prepared to fly at 6:00 AM.
That was a shock.
And so between 4:00 and 6:00 AM, I and all those GIs-- I was the only woman going on that plane-- we all were briefed on donning a parachute and a Mae West life jacket.
And I was very, very tired.

And you know, the-- just the orientation about-- and having to put on a life jacket and a parachute and learn how to do it and everything that would be required in the event of an emergency was pretty tough.
So the plane was loaded on time at 6:00 AM on Military Sea 54, propeller-driven plane.
And we took off and-- and landed in Reykjavik, Iceland.
And we were detained there a half day for repairs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at Iceland, the Icelandic prime minister's wife got on board.
So there were two of us women on that plane loaded with GIs.
And then [? you know ?] hours out over the North Atlantic, an emergency developed.
And we weren't told what it was, but we all did exactly as we were instructed.
Get the life jackets on and prepare to ditch in the North Atlantic.
I didn't know what ditching meant.
I didn't know that you could live in the North Atlantic for exactly one minute.
You know.
However, we-- we did that.
And we were all seated there in our life jackets for hours and hours, knowing nothing other than we were still airborne.
And I don't even remember, you know, the time of day that it was, whether we were going to be ditching in daylight or darkness.
Nobody was speaking.
All we were doing-- I know all I was doing-- was praying.
And then I do remember it was daylight when we came over Ireland, because we were told that it was Ireland.
And it was so green.
The whole country was so green.
And we were so glad to see land, you know, that I have said that we almost didn't care if we crashed then, because it was-- it was land.
And then we were able to continue on to Paris.
And we landed safely after 22 hours in the air.
And it was at that time that we were told that the problem had been a fuel shortage.
Now, it wasn't weeks before my flight that two other Military C54s bound from Westover to Paris loaded with GIs had crashed, one in Newfoundland and one in the Azores.
And we were very aware of that.
We were flight three of that infamous trio of bad luck accidents.
And all those GIs on both flights were killed.
Everybody on those planes was killed.
So we landed in Paris.
[SIGHS] Well, you know, October 31 was the Halloween party.
That's all-- or-- or, 30-- I don't know.
We landed in Paris, I presume, maybe about November 2.

And we were billeted-- I was billeted in the hotel around the Champs-Elysees somewhere with others, because we had to wait for the continuation of our trip on a military C-- a military small plane, C47.
And it was about two-- two days before.
